text: The hiatal hernia is visible at the top of the screen, showing the protrusion of the stomach through the diaphragm into the chest cavity.
The surgeon uses a cautery instrument to dissect fatty tissue that has been pulled out of the hernia sac, sealing the tissue with electricity during the dissection process.
The surgeon continues to dissect additional fatty tissue away from the hernia sac, preparing the area for the subsequent repair.
The robotic surgical system precisely dissects tissue around the hiatal hernia with minimal bleeding, providing clear visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a cautery instrument to cauterize and seal tiny blood vessels, effectively preventing any bleeding during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection around both sides of the esophagus, carefully avoiding injury to the visible nerve structures adjacent to the esophagus.
The nerve is visible at the top of the screen, highlighted within the surgical field.
The robotic system is used to mobilize the esophagus, allowing an instrument to be placed behind it, preparing the area for the subsequent wrap placement.
The diaphragm crura, left and right, are visible bordering the hiatal hernia, and these muscles will later be closed with sutures.
At the top of the dissection field, positioned at twelve o'clock, the surgeon identifies the pulsating white vessel, which is the aorta.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the small blood vessels known as the short gastric vessels located on the outside of the stomach, simultaneously sealing and cutting the tissue.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the upper portion of the stomach, both anteriorly and posteriorly, creating space behind the stomach necessary for the upcoming fundoplication wrap.
The surgeon continues the dissection, carefully dividing blood vessels attached to the spleen and surrounding fatty tissue using a vessel sealer, achieving minimal blood loss.
The robotic instruments demonstrate wristing movements as they maneuver around the tissue, showcasing angles and flexibility not achievable with traditional laparoscopic repair.
Both sides of the stomach are visualized as they are passed behind the esophagus, forming a wrap. The surgeon prepares to close the diaphragm crura using sutures.
The surgeon carefully re-approximates the muscle tissue using sutures, ensuring the tissue is not crushed. A few sutures are placed to close the defect and eliminate the hernia.
The surgeon closes larger defects using sutures and reinforces them with mesh prosthetic material, although this reinforcement is not required for the current case.
